briefing.opening: Hello everyone. Let's begin the climate briefing for May 28, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Emergency and Intensification of Extreme Weather Events
sections.content: Today, significant concerns have been reported globally regarding record-breaking heatwaves and the direct impacts of climate change. Europe and the UK experienced 'staggering' May heatwaves, with indoor temperatures approaching 40°C, leading to a surge in air conditioner sales [35, 54, 61, 71, 78]. The UN warned of record heatwaves as El Niño collides with climate change, and Hajj pilgrims are facing extended periods of exposure to deadly heat [36, 38, 65, 70]. Furthermore, research suggests that large and destructive hailstorms could become more frequent and dangerous due to climate change, and extreme weather events, such as deadly rains in China, are intensifying globally [43, 47, 77].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Response Efforts and Expansion of Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Domestically, efforts to respond to climate change and practice carbon neutrality are actively underway. Wonju City has initiated activities to prevent heat-related illnesses among farmers, and Seoul City is proactively responding to summer heatwaves with a 1.3 billion won project [2, 7]. Seongbuk-gu is fully operating its 'Climate Crisis Disaster Response Enhancement Project,' and the Gwangju-Daegu Heatwave Response Moonlight Forum was held, continuing community-level joint responses and explorations of cooperation measures for the climate crisis [8, 15]. Furthermore, the Korea Climate Change Institute is training carbon neutrality experts, and family-oriented carbon neutrality education and experience programs are gaining significant popularity in Seongdong-gu and Eunpyeong-gu, encouraging citizen participation [4, 10, 11, 13, 20, 21]. Pohang City has shown efforts to strengthen its international climate leadership by pursuing the hosting of the UN Climate Change Conference (COP) [5].

sections.title: ⚠️ Realities of Renewable Energy Transition and Policy Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is accelerating both domestically and internationally, but it faces several challenges. Korea Southern Power (KOSPO) plans to increase its renewable energy facilities tenfold and supply 11.2 GW by 2040, and corporate investments are continuing, with Hyosung Heavy Industries expanding renewable energy use by 30% through the introduction of PPAs [23, 31, 33]. Analysis also suggests that Japan could achieve 90% renewable energy by 2040 more affordably than the government's current plans [25]. However, in Jeju Island, the 'Jeju Paradox' has emerged, where grid instability occurs despite high renewable energy generation, and concerns also exist that the expansion of solar power generation could lead to electricity bill increases [28, 29]. Meanwhile, criticism has been raised that the National Assembly is delaying carbon neutrality-related legislation, and international debates over the direction of energy policy are intensifying, with former Prime Minister Tony Blair advocating for abandoning net-zero targets and expanding fossil fuel drilling [18, 73].

sections.title: 🌍 Widespread Impacts of Climate Change and International Community's Perception
sections.content: Climate change is not merely a meteorological phenomenon; it is shaking geopolitical order and exerting widespread impacts across ecosystems and society as a whole [1, 39]. Research indicating a sharp increase in the climate change sensitivity of Antarctic ice since the last glacial transition suggests long-term threats [42], and impacts on the agricultural sector are becoming a reality, such as wild blueberry farms in Maine suffering damage due to changes in cultivation periods [46]. Furthermore, rice cultivation is increasingly contributing to climate change, and warnings have been issued that microplastics could weaken the ocean's capacity to respond to climate change [49, 62]. Notably, data has been released indicating that public perception of climate change remains divided, with many Americans either unconvinced that humans are causing climate change or pessimistic about avoiding its worst impacts [37, 41, 44, 45].

sections.title: 🌱 Future-Oriented Response Through Innovation and Adaptation
sections.content: Amidst the threats of climate change, efforts to prepare for the future through innovative technologies and adaptation strategies are ongoing. In France, a project is underway to visualize the impact of climate change on cultural heritage using AI [50], and UL Lafayette's live oak tree research is providing new insights into climate change [53]. Cities like Edinburgh are developing concrete plans to adapt to climate change [60, 68], and Czech scientists are breeding hops that can survive in a hotter Europe, enhancing the agricultural sector's adaptability [76]. In this way, various stakeholders are exploring ways to strengthen climate resilience, such as providing students with tools for action to combat sustainability burnout through education [58] and leading economic adaptation in local communities [64].

briefing.closing: As you have seen in today's briefing, climate change is a global challenge, requiring both scientific advancement and policy efforts simultaneously. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Global heating is making hajj ever more dangerous, report finds
articles.description: Rising heat in Saudi Arabia threatens millions of Muslim pilgrims – but cutting fossil fuels would keep it saferGlobal heating has “fundamentally altered” the climate of Mecca and is exposing millions of hajj pilgrims to extreme and dangerous heat even in months outside summer, new analysis has found.Carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels means scorching temperatures of 40C (104F) are now regularly experienced in May, the study showed. In past decades, such peaks would only have occurred in

summary.phenomenon: In the Mecca region of Saudi Arabia, heatwaves of 40°C (104F) are regularly occurring even in May, a phenomenon that did not occur in months outside of summer in past decades. This is exposing millions of Hajj pilgrims to extreme and dangerous heat.
summary.cause: Global warming caused by carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels has 'fundamentally altered' the climate of Mecca.

articles.description: As summers become hotter, air conditioner sales are booming. If you’re looking to invest, here’s what to considerWhen a heatwave struck the UK this week, Jon Connorton, a software developer, began monitoring temperatures inside his east Hampshire terrace house. With some rooms reaching close to 40C, it was time to deploy the air conditioner. “We just wheel it out in emergencies,” he said. “We were having trouble sleeping.”Connorton and his wife have a portable air conditioner. articles.original_title: Blair’s fossil fuel ideas ‘bizarre’ in face of energy and climate crises, experts say
articles.description: Energy specialists say abandoning net zero and increasing oil and gas drilling would cause more instability for BritonsUK politics live – latest updatesAbandoning net zero and drilling for more oil and gas in the North Sea would be a massive setback for the UK and would not help the economy, leading experts have said in response to claims by the former prime minister Tony Blair.“This is a bizarre intervention to make during the worst May heatwave on record and when the Iran crisis is providing y

summary.phenomenon: Amid a record-breaking May heatwave and the Iran crisis, former Prime Minister Tony Blair advocated for abandoning net-zero targets and increasing oil and gas drilling in the North Sea.
summary.outlook: Energy experts have warned that abandoning net-zero targets and increasing oil and gas drilling would cause significant setbacks for the UK, fail to boost the economy, and lead to greater instability.

articles.title: The race for oil: will Jamaica be the next country to drill and what does that mean for its green pledges?
articles.description: With early tests suggesting the presence of crude oil, the Caribbean island has begun to debate whether it could justify becoming a producerJamaica is closer than ever to drilling for oil. Tests on samples from the seabed off the Caribbean island’s south coast earlier this year identified hydrocarbons, which suggest the presence of crude oil below ground.Jamaica imports all its fuel, which costs about $1.5-2bn (£1.1bn-1.5bn) annually, depending on global oil prices. It is a persistent drag on an

summary.phenomenon: Earlier this year, seabed sample tests off the south coast of the Caribbean island of Jamaica confirmed hydrocarbons, suggesting the potential presence of crude oil, prompting Jamaica to begin discussions on whether to pursue oil drilling.
summary.outlook: Jamaica imports all its fuel, amounting to approximately $1.5-2 billion annually, and oil drilling could impact its reliance on these imports.

articles.original_title: Diphtheria is a disease of poverty that has no place in modern Australia. When we talk about Closing the Gap, this is the gap | Donna Ah Chee
articles.description: For those of us working in Aboriginal health, the outbreak doesn’t come as a surprise. We must invest in housing that keeps remote communities safeThe diphtheria outbreak should shock Australia. Not simply because a disease once considered virtually eradicated has returned, but because of where it is spreading and why.More than 220 cases have been recorded in 2026, primarily across the Northern Territory and northern Australia. The overwhelming majority of patients are Aboriginal people, includi

summary.phenomenon: In 2026, over 220 cases of diphtheria were recorded in Australia, primarily in the Northern Territory and northern Australia, with the majority of patients being Aboriginal people.

articles.original_title: ‘Saaz is our gold’: the Czech scientists breeding hops that can survive a hotter Europe
articles.description: Researchers are working to create new drought-resistant varieties of the ingredient that gives Czech pilsner its characterIt is the country that drinks more beer per capita than any other but in the last few years Czechia has been hit by droughts and heatwaves, which make it harder to grow the Saaz hops, one of the key ingredients that goes into the country’s world famous beer.At the Hop Research Institute, however, scientists are working to create new, climate-resilient hop varieties that have

summary.phenomenon: In recent years, Czechia has been affected by droughts and heatwaves, making it difficult to cultivate Saaz hops, a key ingredient in the country's world-famous beer.
summary.outlook: Scientists at the Hop Research Institute are working to develop new climate-resilient hop varieties to ensure hop cultivation can continue even in Europe's increasingly warmer environment.
